game.opening: Queen's Pawn Game (A41) · French-style setup with 2…e6

engine.maia: Maia-1 rating-band networks 1100-1900 via zerofish WASM lc0, nodes=1

accuracy.method: mean of volatility-weighted mean and harmonic mean of per-move accuracies (Lichess formulas)

mistakes.title: The bishop landed on the knight's square
mistakes.explanation: You were still winning comfortably; the only real problem in the position was Black's bishop on b2 attacking your a1-rook. 33.Rab1 solves it by hitting the bishop right back — after 33…Bxc3 34.Rbc1 it doesn't get out. Instead 33.Bh5 attacked the f7-queen, but h5 is exactly the square the f6-knight covers: 33…Nxh5 took the bishop for free and dissolved the "threat" in the same move. Worse, the capture activated the knight — two moves later it sat on f4 guarding g2, and it became the mating piece.

takeaways.lesson: Before releasing a piece, name every enemy piece that touches its new square.
takeaways.detail: Knight coverage is the classic blind spot because it doesn't lie along any line. Make it mechanical: for each candidate move, scan for enemy knights an L-jump away from the target square — here f6 to h5 — before you commit.

takeaways.lesson: When you're winning, solve your problem before creating a threat.
takeaways.detail: The position asked one question — the b2-bishop hitting a1 — and 33.Rab1 answered it. A threat your opponent can meet with a capture is not a threat. When ahead, prefer the move that removes counterplay over the one that attacks.

mistakes.title: Took the bishop, dropped the diagonal, walked into mate
mistakes.explanation: You were lost by now, but 35.Kh1! still fights: it steps off the open g-file, and your queen on b1 keeps guarding g6 along the b1–g6 diagonal, so 35…Qg6 just loses the queen to 36.Qxg6 — the engine's line runs 35…Bxa1 36.Qb4+! with real counterplay against Black's own exposed king on f8. 35.Qxb2 pocketed the b2-bishop instead, and the diagonal went with it: 35…Qg6+ — unanswerable now — 36.Kh1 Qg2#, with the knight you had allowed onto f4 guarding the mating square.

takeaways.lesson: Before grabbing material, run through every check your opponent gets in reply.
takeaways.detail: Make the forcing scan a fixed pre-capture habit: their checks first, then captures, then threats. With your king on an open g-file and a knight parked on f4, …Qg6+ deserved five seconds of your time before anything else.

takeaways.lesson: Ask what a piece is defending before you move it.
takeaways.detail: The b1-queen wasn't idle — it was the only piece covering the g6 entry square. Every time you move a piece, especially the queen, name the squares it stops defending; "free" material that pulls a defender off duty is the oldest trap in the game.

mistakes.title: Two pieces on one pawn's path
mistakes.explanation: Out of the opening you had everything: pawns on d4 and e5, extra space, and Black's king already stuck on f8. 8.Ne4 jumped into the centre — onto a square the d6-pawn attacks with a single step. 8…d5! forked the e4-knight and the c4-bishop, and there is no save: 9.Bd3 dxe4 10.Bxe4 leaves you a knight for a pawn down. A quiet move like 8.Qe2 (or 8.h4) keeps the bind and the advantage. The c4-bishop had been standing in the d-pawn's path since move 7 — placing a second piece on that same path turned a nuisance into a fork.

takeaways.lesson: Before centralizing a piece, check which enemy pawn advances hit the new square.
takeaways.detail: A pawn attacks the two diagonal squares one step ahead, so every pawn push is a potential fork. Before a knight or bishop lands, ask: can a pawn step forward and touch it — and what else would that push attack?

takeaways.lesson: Don't park two pieces where one pawn move attacks both.
takeaways.detail: c4 and e4 are both hit by a pawn arriving on d5. When two of your pieces stand a single pawn advance apart like that, the fork is loaded even if it can't fire yet — reroute one of them first.

mistakes.title: b7: one square in, no squares out
mistakes.explanation: 9…b6? attacked your c5-knight but handed you a clean exit: 10.Nxd7+! — a capture with check, thanks to the king Black had parked on f8 — trades the doomed knight for the d7-bishop, and after 10…Qxd7 11.Bb5! the c4-bishop escapes the d5-pawn with tempo on the queen. You would have stood clearly better. Instead 10.Nb7 hit the d8-queen, but b7 is a dead end: b6 covers both a5 and c5, the e7-bishop covers d6 and d8, and after the engine's 10…Qc8! followed by …Qxb7 the knight is simply lost. Black let you off with 10…Qb8 11.Ba6, and 12…Na5?? later turned the stranded knight into a free capture — that rescue was Black's gift, not the move's merit.

takeaways.lesson: Count a knight's retreat squares before it jumps in — zero means it's lost.
takeaways.detail: Deep in the enemy camp, list the knight's exits and who covers them. From b7 every square — a5, c5, d6, d8 — was controlled. If the count is zero and no tactic bails it out, the knight is dead the moment it lands.

takeaways.lesson: When a piece is attacked, check its captures first — a trade with check is the cleanest save.
takeaways.detail: An attacked piece doesn't have to run; it can cash itself in. Nxd7+ forced the recapture and even gained a tempo for the bishop afterwards. Scan the forcing exits — captures and checks — before you look at retreats.

mistakes.title: The queen stepped into the c-pawn's headlights
mistakes.explanation: A full knight up after the b7 adventure ended in your favour, you only needed to consolidate — the engine's 15.a4 grabs queenside space without giving Black a target. 15.Qd3 put the queen one step from Black's c-pawn: 15…c4! comes with tempo, drives her straight back, and permanently shuts your b3-knight behind the pawn. Half the advantage evaporated on a single pawn move. Black delayed it once, but …c4 arrived anyway (16…c4 17.Qc2), and that knight spent the entire rest of the game shuffling between b3 and d2 — it was still standing on d2, watching, when the mate landed on g2.

takeaways.lesson: Never post the queen where a pawn can hit her in one move.
takeaways.detail: A pawn attacking a queen always wins a tempo, and the pawn usually improves its own position for free. Before every queen move, scan the enemy pawns one and two squares away from her new post.

takeaways.lesson: When you're a piece up, watch for pawn moves that bury your pieces.
takeaways.detail: …c4 didn't win material — it entombed the b3-knight, handing the piece back positionally. Material only counts if it can move; check which of your pieces a pawn advance could lock out before you allow it.
